summary: Shapiro discusses the awkward situation of being asked for a business plan by investors, referencing Brad Feld and Jason Mendelson's book 'Venture Deals' which acknowledges the document is largely obsolete but warns founders to be prepared for the request. Drawing from his experience pitching 70+ VCs and 100+ angels across multiple funding rounds at Ontela, Shapiro notes that no VC ever asked for a business plan, but several angels did during the seed round. His solution: send the same materials you send everyone else—an executive summary and slide deck—without apology or acknowledgment. He notes that investors who ask for business plans are probably not the right fit for high-growth tech startups, as none of the angels who requested one ultimately invested.
key_points: Business plans are effectively obsolete in venture financing—top VCs haven't read one in decades, preferring demos and live interactions
When asked for a business plan, simply send your standard pitch materials without apology or mention of the business plan request
The business plan request came only from angels during seed rounds, never from VCs during Series A or B
Investors who request business plans are a signal of misalignment—none of the ones who asked ultimately invested
Founders often feel guilty or scared when asked for something they skipped, but the 'foolproof solution' is to not treat it as a gap
money_quotes: Whenever an investor asks you for your business plan, send them the same damn packet you send to everyone else.
Don't apologize and don't mention the business plan.
We did this at least a dozen times and had precisely zero complaints.
Investors who want business plans are probably not your target market, if you're founding a high growth technology startup.
We had lots of great followup conversations with the angels who wanted them, but ultimately none of them turned in to investors.
There was a lot of hemming and hawing before we figured out a foolproof solution.
key_insight: When investors ask for a business plan, don't panic or apologize—just send your standard pitch materials, and recognize that the request itself is a signal of investor-founder misalignment.
